content.text: Golden Record Strategy for GenAI Context Data

A RAG system injects context into the LLM prompt from multiple sources: retrieved document chunks, user profile data, session history, entity metadata, and structural knowledge like org charts. Without a golden record strategy for these context data types, the same entity (a product, a customer, an org unit) can appear with conflicting attributes depending on which system supplied the context — leading to responses that contradict each other across turns or across users.

attrs.alt: Golden record strategy for GenAI context data

content.text: Context Data Types That Need Golden Records
Context Type
Examples
MDM Risk Without Golden Record
Staleness Window
Entity data
Product catalogue, customer records, employee directory
Different CRM and ERP supply conflicting price or status
Minutes to hours
User profile
Role, department, consent state, RAG scope
Old role used for access decisions after org change
Near real-time
Session context
Prior turns, retrieved chunk IDs, cited sources
Duplicate or contradictory prior answers injected
Session-scoped (ephemeral)
Structural metadata
Org hierarchy, team membership, reporting lines
Escalation paths based on outdated reporting structure
Daily to weekly
Domain knowledge graph
Entity relationships, product-regulatory mappings
Outdated regulation cited as current for a product
Weekly to monthly
Grounding sources
SourceRecord from previous sub-subtopic
Duplicate ingestion with conflicting freshness clocks
Hours
Golden Entity Record

For business entities that appear in queries (products, customers, accounts, employees), the golden record is the single authoritative representation, sourced from the system of record and cached for rapid context injection.

from __future__ import annotations
import enum
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any, Optional

class EntityType(enum.Enum):
    PRODUCT   = "product"
    CUSTOMER  = "customer"
    EMPLOYEE  = "employee"
    ORG_UNIT  = "org_unit"
    ACCOUNT   = "account"

@dataclass
class EntitySourceRef:
    system: str          # e.g. "crm", "erp", "hr_system"
    external_id: str     # native ID in that system
    last_synced_at: datetime

@dataclass
class GoldenEntityRecord:
    """Single canonical representation of a business entity for context injection."""
    golden_id: str
    entity_type: EntityType
    canonical_name: str
    attributes: dict[str, Any]       # e.g. {"status": "active", "price_usd": 299.99}
    source_refs: list[EntitySourceRef]
    classification_label: str         # data classification of this entity's attributes
    permitted_apps: list[str]
    created_at: datetime
    last_updated_at: datetime
    cache_ttl_seconds: int = 300      # how long downstream systems may cache this record

    def is_stale(self) -> bool:
        age = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - self.last_updated_at
        return age > timedelta(seconds=self.cache_ttl_seconds)

    def to_context_snippet(self) -> str:
        """Format the entity for injection into a prompt context window."""
        attrs = "; ".join(f"{k}={v}" for k, v in self.attributes.items())
        return f"[{self.entity_type.value.upper()}] {self.canonical_name} ({attrs})"
Entity Resolution: Matching Mentions to Golden Records

When a user query mentions "the product" or "our Q1 pricing", the context assembler must resolve these references to specific golden entity records before injecting context. Entity resolution uses three strategies in order.
Golden Record Context Store

The context store caches golden records for rapid retrieval during query processing. It enforces TTL-based staleness — a record past its TTL is re-fetched from the system of record before being injected into the prompt.
Context Assembler — Merging Golden Context

The context assembler combines retrieved document chunks with resolved entity records and user profile data into the final context window. Each source is stamped with its golden ID and last-updated timestamp so the LLM can reason about recency without the application needing to hard-code freshness logic in the prompt.

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class ContextItem:
    source_type: str        # "document_chunk" | "entity" | "user_profile" | "session_turn"
    golden_id: str
    content: str
    last_updated_at: str    # ISO 8601
    classification_label: str

class ContextAssembler:
    def __init__(
        self,
        entity_store: GoldenEntityStore,
        retriever,
        entity_resolver: EntityResolver,
        max_tokens: int = 4096,
    ) -> None:
        self._entities = entity_store
        self._retriever = retriever
        self._resolver = entity_resolver
        self._max_tokens = max_tokens

    def assemble(
        self,
        query: str,
        user_id: str,
        user_profile: dict[str, Any],
        session_turns: list[dict],
        app_id: str,
    ) -> list[ContextItem]:
        items: list[ContextItem] = []

        # 1. User profile context
        items.append(ContextItem(
            source_type="user_profile",
            golden_id=f"user:{user_id}",
            content=f"User role={user_profile.get('role')} dept={user_profile.get('department')}",
            last_updated_at=user_profile.get("updated_at", "unknown"),
            classification_label="internal",
        ))

        # 2. Entity mentions resolved from query
        words = query.split()
        for mention in words:
            match = self._resolver.resolve(mention)
            if match:
                snippet = self._entities.get_for_context(match.golden_id, app_id)
                if snippet:
                    entity = self._entities.get(match.golden_id)
                    items.append(ContextItem(
                        source_type="entity",
                        golden_id=match.golden_id,
                        content=snippet,
                        last_updated_at=entity.last_updated_at.isoformat(),
                        classification_label=entity.classification_label,
                    ))

        # 3. Retrieved document chunks
        chunks = self._retriever.retrieve(query, subject_id=user_id, top_k=5)
        for chunk in chunks:
            meta = chunk.get("metadata", {})
            items.append(ContextItem(
                source_type="document_chunk",
                golden_id=meta.get("golden_id", meta.get("doc_id", "unknown")),
                content=chunk.get("content", ""),
                last_updated_at=meta.get("source_updated_at", "unknown"),
                classification_label=meta.get("classification_label", "internal"),
            ))

        return items   # caller trims to max_tokens
Survivorship Rules for Context Conflicts

When the same entity attribute appears in both a retrieved document and the entity golden record, the golden record wins — it is the system-of-record value. Documents may reflect the state at time of writing; the golden record reflects now.
Conflict Scenario
Resolution
Rationale
Document says product price=$299; golden record says $349
Use $349 from golden record
Golden record is live; document reflects publication date
Document says employee is in team A; org chart golden record says team B
Use team B from golden record
Org chart is the system of record for reporting structure
Two retrieved chunks give different regulatory deadlines
Surface both with their source timestamps; let LLM note the discrepancy
Neither chunk is the system of record; escalate to human review
User profile says preferred language=EN; session turn written in FR
Use EN for response; acknowledge session language context
User profile is the persistent preference; session language may be transient
Staleness Management for Context Records
Entity cache TTL by type.

Product pricing: 60 s. Employee role: 5 min. Org hierarchy: 1 h. Regulatory mappings: 24 h. Longer TTLs trade freshness for latency; set them based on how fast the source system changes, not on retrieval convenience.
Invalidation on source events.

When the system of record emits a change event (product price updated, employee offboarded), publish it to the context store's invalidation channel. The cache removes the stale entry immediately rather than waiting for TTL expiry.
Stale-read guard.
If a golden record's TTL has expired and the system of record is unreachable, return the stale record with a
is_stale=True
flag. The context assembler includes a staleness warning in the prompt so the LLM can qualify its answer.
Summary

A golden record strategy for GenAI context data extends MDM beyond source documents to cover the full set of information injected into the context window: business entity records, user profiles, structural metadata, and domain knowledge graph facts. Entity resolution maps query mentions to stable golden IDs; the context store enforces TTL-based freshness; and survivorship rules ensure that when a retrieved document conflicts with the system-of-record value, the golden record wins. Together these mechanisms prevent the context window from carrying internally inconsistent information — the single most common source of factual contradictions in enterprise RAG responses.
